Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed divine-agent clause: divine logogram subject + possessed/body-part locative + finite verb phrase; gnomic present sense.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A powerful protector or lucky circumstance shields someone at the decisive moment.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: He or she is protected by strong support or good fortune.
Usage Contexts: political praise; military narrative; family encouragement; ritual blessing
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom grounded in Storm-God royal-protection imagery common in Anatolian monumental ideology; not an attested formula.
First Example Sentence: a-wa/i za-ti DIES-ni TONITRUS-sa MANUS-ti tar-pa-na-ti.
Second Example Sentence: REX-ti ARHA i-ia-ta, wa/i TONITRUS-sa MANUS-ti tar-pa-na-ti.
Third Example Sentence: INFANS-ni ni ḫwa/i-ti; TONITRUS-sa MANUS-ti tar-pa-na-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative gnomic clause: body-part subject + road locative + negator + finite verb; proverbial aphorism.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Actions and traces reveal the truth even when speech conceals it.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom inspired by travel, border, and campaign motifs in Hieroglyphic Luwian inscriptions; not an attested idiom.
First Example Sentence: tu-wa/i wa/i-ta-ni la-ti, PES-sa VIA-ti ni wa/i-la-ti.
Second Example Sentence: SCRIBA-li TABULA-ni ša-na-ti, wa/i PES-sa VIA-ti ni wa/i-la-ti.
Third Example Sentence: ARHA i-ia-ta-sa ku-wa/i-ti? PES-sa VIA-ti ni wa/i-la-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Inanimate-agent proverb: food noun as subject + house locative + speech/declare verb; metonymic construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Hospitality proves goodwill more strongly than promises or formal speeches.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Generous actions show kindness better than words.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom based on ancient Near Eastern hospitality values and household imagery; not an attested Hieroglyphic Luwian expression.
Second Example Sentence: FEMINA-ti PANIS-ni pi-ia-ti, wa/i PANIS-sa DOMUS-ni tara/i-ti.
Third Example Sentence: ni MAGNUS wa/i-ta-ni; PANIS-sa DOMUS-ni tara/i-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Agentive nominal clause: professional subject + direct object + cognition verb; compact expert-proverb structure.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A trained person notices details that others miss.
Usage Contexts: education; administration; archive work; technical review
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom reflecting scribal administration and inscriptional record-keeping in Neo-Hittite states; not an attested phrase.
Second Example Sentence: a-pa-sa ni wa/i-la-ti, wa/i SCRIBA-li TABULA-ni ša-na-ti.
Third Example Sentence: TABULA-ni MALUS-ti i-zi-ia-ta; SCRIBA-li TABULA-ni ša-na-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Stative locative clause: throne/authority logogram subject + land locative + stative verb; legitimacy metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Authority is established, recognized, and difficult to dislodge.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: The rule is secure and publicly accepted.
Usage Contexts: politics; royal ideology; legal confirmation; historical commentary
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom inspired by throne, land, and kingship formulas typical of monumental royal display; not an attested idiom.
First Example Sentence: REX-ti ni ḫwa/i-ti; SOLIUM-sa REGIO-ni á-sa-ti.
Second Example Sentence: URBS-ni BONUS-ti paḫ-sa-ti, wa/i SOLIUM-sa REGIO-ni á-sa-ti.
Third Example Sentence: REL-sa tar-pa-na-ti, a-pa-ti SOLIUM-sa REGIO-ni á-sa-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Idiomatic infinitival/finite action phrase: gate locative + outward particle + motion verb; risk-taking metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To leave safety and act publicly, often under scrutiny.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: To step into public responsibility or danger.
Usage Contexts: leadership; public speech; rites of passage; conflict mediation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom modeled on city-gate and public-space symbolism in ancient Anatolian urban life; not an attested phrase.
First Example Sentence: tu-wa/i PORTA-ti ARHA i-ia-ti, wa/i URBS-ni ša-na-ti.
Second Example Sentence: INFANS-sa MAGNUS-ti a-sa-ta; PORTA-ti ARHA i-ia-ti.
Third Example Sentence: ni ḫwa/i-ti, REX-ti PORTA-ti ARHA i-ia-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Object-locative verb phrase: stele locative + word/speech object + guarding verb; archival-memory metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To preserve a statement, law, or reputation so it cannot be easily erased.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: To record and protect the message.
Usage Contexts: law; inscriptions; archives; public memory; treaty language
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom drawn from the public permanence of stone inscriptions; Hieroglyphic Luwian survives mainly in inscriptional contexts, but this is not an attested idiom.
Second Example Sentence: MALUS wa/i-ta-ni ni a-sa-ti; STELE-ni wa/i-ta-ni paḫ-sa-ti.
Third Example Sentence: REGIO-sa ḫwa/i-ti, wa/i REX-ti STELE-ni wa/i-ta-ni paḫ-sa-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personified inanimate clause: river subject + city locative/dative + speech verb; omen-warning construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Warning signs are already visible before a crisis becomes obvious.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: The situation itself is warning the people.
Usage Contexts: environment; politics; military preparation; family caution
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom inspired by river valleys, frontier cities, and omen-like historical narration in Anatolian and Syrian contexts; not attested.
First Example Sentence: AQUA-sa MAGNUS-ti u-wa/i-ti; FLUMEN-sa URBS-ni wa/i-ia-ti.
Second Example Sentence: SCRIBA-li ni ḫwa/i-ti, wa/i FLUMEN-sa URBS-ni wa/i-ia-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Dative-recipient construction: child/young person dative + adjective + speech object + giving verb; burden metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To give someone too much responsibility, praise, or expectation before they are ready.
Usage Contexts: family; education; succession; mentorship; workplace training
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom tied to succession, apprenticeship, and dynastic training themes; not an attested expression.
Second Example Sentence: tu-wa/i ša-na-ši, INFANS-ni MAGNUS wa/i-ta-ni pi-ia-ti?
Third Example Sentence: REX-ti INFANS-ni MAGNUS wa/i-ta-ni pi-ia-ti, wa/i REGIO-sa ta-ru/i-ti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative locative-status clause: royal title/agent + throne locative + negator + stative verb; irony of empty authority.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone has the title or appearance of power but lacks real control.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: The person is not truly in charge.
Usage Contexts: politics; satire; workplace hierarchy; court narrative
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic pedagogical idiom based on royal titulature and throne imagery; intentionally crafted for sentiment, sarcasm, and authority modeling, not attested.
First Example Sentence: a-pa-sa REX-ti wa/i-la-ti, wa/i REX-ti SOLIUM-ni ni á-sa-ti.
Second Example Sentence: URBS-ni SCRIBA-li paḫ-sa-ti; REX-ti SOLIUM-ni ni á-sa-ti.
Third Example Sentence: MAGNUS wa/i-ta-ni a-sa-ti, wa/i REX-ti SOLIUM-ni ni á-sa-ti.
